readings.title: Reading 1
readings.text:
It happened that while Apollos was in Corinth, Paul made his way overland as far as Ephesus, where he found a number of disciples.
When he asked, 'Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you became believers?' they answered, 'No, we were never even told there was such a thing as a Holy Spirit.'
He asked, 'Then how were you baptised?' They replied, 'With John's baptism.'
Paul said, 'John's baptism was a baptism of repentance; but he insisted that the people should believe in the one who was to come after him -- namely Jesus.'
When they heard this, they were baptised in the name of the Lord Jesus,
and the moment Paul had laid hands on them the Holy Spirit came down on them, and they began to speak with tongues and to prophesy.
There were about twelve of these men in all.
He began by going to the synagogue, where he spoke out fearlessly and argued persuasively about the kingdom of God. He did this for three months,

exegesis.summary: Paul finds twelve disciples at Ephesus who had received only John's baptism of repentance. He completes their initiation by baptizing them in the name of the Lord Jesus and laying hands on them, and the Holy Spirit descends with the gifts of tongues and prophecy. Paul then enters the synagogue and argues boldly about the kingdom of God for three months.

patristicQuotes.quote: The baptism of Christ does not undo the baptism of John, but includes it in itself. The Apostle, having found certain Ephesians baptized with John's baptism, baptized them again in the name of Christ, because they had not been baptized in the Spirit. They were not baptized twice but once, for the baptism of Christ is something new, not the same repeated.

Our Lord retains in His own possession exclusively the power of baptism, while the ministering of it passes to good and bad alike. It is by this power that the unity of the Church is established, of which it is said, 'My dove is one.' What hindrance is the badness of the minister, when the Lord is good?

cccReferences.title: From Pentecost onward the Church baptizes those who believe
Through Baptism the believer is buried and raised with Christ
The Holy Spirit rests on the Messiah and on the baptized
The apostles laid hands so that the baptized would receive the Spirit
John's baptism prepared for the baptism in water and the Spirit

exegesis.thematicConnection: Acts shows the Holy Spirit completing what John's baptism only foreshadowed, fulfilling the Father's presence promised in today's Gospel ('the Father is with me'). The same Spirit who undergirds Christ's victory in John 16 now descends on the twelve at Ephesus, gathering those who had been scattered into the unity of the Church the Psalm celebrates.

readings.title: Responsorial Psalm
readings.text:
You disperse them like smoke; as wax melts in the presence of a fire, so the wicked melt at the presence of God.
The upright rejoice in the presence of God, delighted and crying out for joy.
Sing to God, play music to his name, build a road for the Rider of the Clouds, rejoice in Yahweh, dance before him.
Father of orphans, defender of widows, such is God in his holy dwelling.
God gives the lonely a home to live in, leads prisoners out into prosperity, but rebels must live in the bare wastelands.
God, when you set out at the head of your people, when you strode over the desert,
readings.refrain: You disperse them like smoke; as wax melts in the presence of a fire, so the wicked melt at the presence of God.

exegesis.summary: Psalm 68 sings of God arising in power: the wicked melt before His face like wax in fire, while the upright rejoice with delight. He is named Father of orphans and defender of widows, the One who gives the lonely a home and leads prisoners into freedom. The Lord rides as conqueror at the head of His pilgrim people.

patristicQuotes.quote: God ever appears in a cloud, as the Psalms say: 'clouds and darkness are round about him.' Therefore shall the Son of Man come in the clouds as God and the Lord, not secretly, but in glory worthy of God — with great power and majesty.

The Word of God entered the ship, that is, the Church, and the multitude follow the Lord out of the city into the desert — going from the Synagogue to the Church. The Lord sees them, has compassion, and heals all sickness, cleansing their obstructed minds and unbelieving hearts for the understanding of the new preaching.

cccReferences.title: Cloud and light reveal and veil the saving God
God calls together all men scattered by sin into His family
God's people learn to pray in the shadow of His dwelling
The earthly liturgy is a foretaste of the heavenly liturgy
The angels accompany God when He rides forth at the head of His people

exegesis.thematicConnection: The Psalm gives voice to the Ephesians' joy in Acts: God gives the lonely a home — and twelve disciples who had received only repentance are now brought into the household of the Spirit. It also illumines Jesus' Gospel words: even when His own scatter, God remains the Father of orphans, the gatherer of the abandoned.

readings.title: Gospel
readings.text:
His disciples said, 'Now you are speaking plainly and not using veiled language.
Now we see that you know everything and need not wait for questions to be put into words; because of this we believe that you came from God.'
Jesus answered them: Do you believe at last?
Listen; the time will come -- indeed it has come already -- when you are going to be scattered, each going his own way and leaving me alone. And yet I am not alone, because the Father is with me.
I have told you all this so that you may find peace in me. In the world you will have hardship, but be courageous: I have conquered the world.

exegesis.summary: On the eve of His Passion the disciples claim to understand Jesus clearly at last and profess belief that He came from God. Jesus does not let the confession stand unchallenged: He foretells their scattering, yet declares He will not be alone because the Father is with Him. He gives them His peace and one triumphant word: 'I have conquered the world.'

patristicQuotes.quote: We must not understand 'I am no more in the world' metaphorically of the heart and life — could there ever have been a time when He loved the things of the world? He means rather that He was soon going away. And then He commends to His Father those whom He was about to leave.

He speaks in condescension to the minds of His disciples, who thought they were more safe in His presence. He kept them in the Father's name, that is, by the Father's help — for love is not to be troubled, but to keep His commandments.

He spoke openly to the world, that is, within the hearing of many. So in one sense He spoke openly, in that many heard Him; in another sense not openly, in that they did not understand Him. His speaking apart with His disciples was not speaking in secret.

cccReferences.title: Christ's filial prayer entrusts everything to the Father
Jesus prays alone on the mountain, including all men in His prayer
Christ's whole life is the Revelation of the Father
In the Son the Father has spoken His one and final Word
The Spirit reveals Christ and dwells with those who believe

exegesis.thematicConnection: John 16 supplies the doctrinal floor for the day: the Father is never absent from the Son, and Christ's victory is already accomplished before the Passion has begun. That victory is what gets ratified in Acts when the Spirit descends on the Ephesians, and it is what Psalm 68 sings when God arises and the scattered are gathered home.

synthesis.title: He has conquered the world, and now He fills it with His Spirit
synthesis.body: Today the Church proclaims a single Easter mystery in three voices. In the Gospel, Christ stands on the threshold of His Passion and declares the war already won: 'In the world you will have hardship, but be courageous: I have conquered the world.' The Father has never left Him, even when His own are about to scatter. In Acts, that victory becomes visible at Ephesus. The disciples of John had received only a baptism of repentance, a baptism that pointed beyond itself; Paul completes what was begun, baptizes them into the name of the Lord Jesus, and the Holy Spirit — Christ's own promised gift — descends. What was scattered is gathered; what was incomplete is filled. Psalm 68 supplies the song for both scenes: God arises, His enemies melt like wax, and the lonely are given a home. The Father of orphans is the same Father who is with Jesus in His hour and with the twelve at Ephesus in theirs. The Church does not yet see the conquered world with her eyes, but she already sings the victory with her voice, because the One who said 'I have conquered' has poured His Spirit into her heart.

questions: Where am I in the Bible story? You are in the upper room in Jerusalem, late on the night before Jesus dies. The Passover meal is over. Judas has already slipped out into the dark. Jesus has been speaking for a long time — about the Father, the Vine, the Spirit who is coming — and now He finally speaks plainly, with no parables. In one hour they will leave for the Garden of Gethsemane.

Who is present? Jesus, fully aware He is about to be arrested. Eleven disciples, exhausted, full of wine and food and big emotions, finally feeling like they understand. They are hopeful, a little proud of themselves, and completely unaware of what the next twelve hours will demand.

What is the main tension? The disciples think they have arrived at faith. Jesus knows they have not. He is about to be alone in a way they cannot imagine, and they are about to fail in a way they cannot imagine. The tension is the gap between what they believe of themselves and what is about to be true.

What is God revealing about Himself? That the Father is never absent from the Son, even when every human being abandons Him. That Jesus' victory does not depend on His disciples' faithfulness. That He gives peace as a gift, not as a reward for performance.

What does this passage show me about the human heart? That we often confess Christ most loudly right before we abandon Him. That our faith is real but smaller than we think. That Jesus loves us with full knowledge of what we are about to do, not in spite of it.

What word or phrase stands out? 'I am not alone, because the Father is with me.' Jesus speaks the antidote to every loneliness ever felt by a Christian — He shares the Father's presence with us through the same Spirit that holds Him.

What is God asking of me today? To trust that He has already conquered the world, even when my own corner of it looks unconquered. To accept His peace as a gift on Monday morning instead of earning it by Friday.

How does this connect to Jesus? This IS Jesus — His own voice on the eve of His Passion, naming the victory before the cross, refusing to let suffering have the final word. Every Catholic stands inside this hour every time we say 'peace be with you' at Mass.
How can I pray this passage? 'Jesus, courage. You have conquered the world. Give me peace in You today, not when the trouble lifts.'
What truth do I carry today? He has already won. My job is to walk with the Victor, not to win the war myself.

gospelImmersion._source: step-2-reading-bible skill (Fr. Mike Schmitz Ignatian method)
gospelImmersion.icbParaphrase: The disciples said, 'Now You are speaking plainly. You are not hiding anything.' Then they said, 'Now we see You know everything. We believe You came from God.' Jesus answered them, 'Do you really believe now? Listen. The time is coming — it is here already — when you will all run away. You will leave Me alone. But I am not alone. The Father is with Me. I told you these things so you can have peace in Me. In the world you will have trouble. But be brave. I have won. I have beaten the world.'

senses.sight: You are in a low-ceilinged upper room. Oil lamps flicker against rough plastered walls. The long table is still cluttered — cups, broken pieces of unleavened bread, a smear of red wine. Eleven men lean toward Jesus, their faces flushed from food and feeling. Jesus sits at the head, very still. His sleeves are still damp at the wrists from washing their feet earlier. His eyes are dark and steady. Outside the small high window the Jerusalem night is black and very quiet.
senses.sound: The room has gone hushed after a long, intense conversation. You hear the soft hiss of the lamp wicks. A cup is set down on wood. Someone's robe rustles as he leans in. When the disciples speak, they almost interrupt each other in their excitement — 'Now You are speaking plainly!' — their voices rising. Then Jesus answers, and His voice is lower than theirs, slower, almost tender, as if He is breaking news gently to people who do not yet know they need to hear it.
senses.touch: The bench under you is hard and warm from sitting. The night air coming in through the window is cool on the back of your neck. Your sandals are off; the floor is cold stone through your feet. You can feel that your stomach is full and your eyelids are heavy. If you reach out and touch the table, the wood is sticky from spilled wine. Your hand, when you set it down, almost touches the edge of Jesus' cloak.
senses.smell: Lamp oil, faintly bitter. The yeasty, charred smell of bread crusts. Sour wine. The clean sharpness of the bowl of water still in the corner where Jesus knelt to wash feet. Underneath it all, the cool dust-and-stone smell of an old Jerusalem house at night.
senses.taste: A little wine still on your lips. The papery aftertaste of unleavened bread. A trace of bitter herbs from the Passover meal. Mostly, the strange dry taste of a mouth that has been listening for hours without speaking.

gospelImmersion.jesusMoment: Jesus is leaning forward, elbows on the table, fingers laced. The lamp catches one side of His face and leaves the other in shadow. He is not angry that the disciples have spoken too confidently — He looks at them the way a parent looks at a child who has said 'I'll be fine, Mama' on the first day of school. His eyes go around the table, settling on each of them in turn. When His gaze reaches you, it does not move on quickly. He looks at you for a long moment, knowing exactly what you will do tomorrow, this week, this year — and the look does not change. Then He smiles, just barely, and says quietly, only to you: 'Be courageous. I have conquered the world.'

rosaryTieIn.reasonForToday: Monday is the Church's traditional day for the Joyful Mysteries — the coming of Christ into the world that He, in today's Gospel, declares He has already conquered.
rosaryTieIn.spiritualFruit: Humility, charity, poverty of spirit, obedience, and the joy of finding Jesus when He seems hidden.
rosaryTieIn.connectionToGospel: In John 16, Christ stands on the edge of His Passion and says, 'I am not alone, the Father is with Me' — the same Father who in the Joyful Mysteries sent Him into the world through Mary's yes. The Joyful Mysteries show us why Jesus could speak with such peace tonight: He has known the Father's presence since the moment of the Annunciation, and that unbroken communion is now the source of His promise of peace to us.

walkWithHim.companionPrompt: Good morning. You are here, in this room with me before I have even gotten up. I do not have to summon You — You stayed the night. Today I will probably forget that more than I remember it, but You told the disciples last night, 'In the world you will have trouble, but be courageous; I have conquered the world.' I am taking that as Yours to me this morning. The trouble is real — I am not pretending it is not — but You have already won, and I get to walk through Monday next to the Victor, not as a soldier still in the fight. Stay close. We have today.
walkWithHim.oneTruthToCarry: He has already conquered the world; I just need to walk through Monday beside Him.
